summary: Gruber analyzes the hardware announcements from WWDC 2006, covering three angles: Apple's surging notebook market share (6% to 12%), the Mac Pro launch, and Apple's naming conventions. He argues notebook market share is a more meaningful metric than overall PC market share for gauging Apple's health, and that Boot Camp is driving switcher sales. He notes the Mac Pro was inevitable but its aggressive pricing was the real surprise. The bulk of the piece dissects Apple's consolidation of its product line under the 'Mac' brand, questioning why Xserve was excluded and why 'iMac' now feels slightly inconsistent.
key_points: Apple's notebook market share doubling from 6% to 12% is the most meaningful indicator of platform health, since notebook buyers are in Apple's actual competitive segment
The Intel switch makes Apple's hardware roadmap predictable, removing the element of surprise from system architecture announcements
The Mac Pro's aggressive pricing was the real surprise — Apple deliberately set out to 'bust the myth' that Macs cost more than comparable PCs
Apple's 'Mac' naming consolidation is complete except for Xserve, likely because Xserve customers may run non-Mac operating systems
The 'i' prefix has drifted from meaning 'Internet' to meaning 'fun consumer-level Apple product,' creating a subtle branding inconsistency with the iMac
money_quotes: All hail the Boot Camp 'even if I don't like the Mac OS I can just install Windows' safety net.
Notebook market share is much more indicative of the overall interest in the Mac platform.
I didn't even list 'Intel-based replacements for the Power Macs' on my WWDC predictions because it seemed too obvious, like predicting that Jobs would be wearing a black shirt.
I don't think anyone would have batted an eyelash if Apple had set the price on these new Mac Pros $500 higher. The actual prices strike me as very aggressive.

While nearly everyone agrees that in and of themselves, 'PowerBook' and 'iBook' are better-looking, better-sounding names than 'MacBook Pro' and 'MacBook', it's better for the overall platform brand to put all the hardware under the 'Mac' umbrella.
It's not like the Xserve is lost in a labyrinthian Dell-like product matrix: Apple sells two notebook brands, three desktops, and one server. That's it.
The 'i' has more or less come to mean 'fun consumer-level product from Apple', which really has no logical connection with the letter 'i'.
key_insight: The Intel transition made Apple's hardware roadmap predictable, shifting the competitive surprise from architecture to pricing and branding — and Apple leveraged both aggressively at WWDC 2006.
